text: To tie this up, I ended up dropping the heatmap vis that was there and just went with a "disappearing key" effects for visualizing the history of the symbols. It was actually sort of interesting how the keyboard is sort of organized in rings with the innermost keys being the oldest and the newest keys being on the periphery. If only there was more time in the day :slightly_smiling_face:

text:
<@UE0ETTCG7>
For value add measurement -  I like these 3 books:- The Mom Test, Microsoft secrets, Competing Against Luck.

For example: Rob Fitzpatrick, bankrupted 3 businesses. Because he did not know how assess the value add of an idea. Also Microsoft had similar problems in the 1990's. People have learned from these failures, and there are now better processes that help with idea validation.

The main idea is to measure the value add of an idea. These 3 books do this in slightly different ways, but they all assess the "value add" by comparing to the current solution. You take the current solution and use that to evaluate new ideas.

Some people call the current solution "As Is" and the proposed solution "To Be".

- Microsoft secrets - It covers many topics and has a chapter or two on idea validation and product road map processes. Some of the product road map processes are aimed at improving existing solutions and large budget project. That said the core lesson is more or less the same.

- Rob Fitzpatrick - book "The Mom Test". Video of him talking about the problem and solution. He describes how to create "as is" user stories so developers can design good "to be" stories. Watch how he picks features. (45 Min) <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0LwbFZkyRKk>

- Clayton Christensen - Competing Against Luck: The Story of Innovation and Customer Choice - is related, the book review I have seen suggest his process is mostly the same, but I have not read it.

Focus groups/user testing/UX - as described by <@UEQ6M68H0> above is also discussed in Microsoft secrets. - In my view, value add and ease of use are not the same. For example, word processors have become easy to use, you don't need an instruction manual or training course these days, but I remember word perfect and having to memorise key combinations for bold and centre etc.   ....  With the problem of value add, you can create a reasonable estimate of the value add of a new feature idea long before you write any code. The 3 books describe ways to do this.

Excel story ... "Over the next two weeks we visited dozens of Excel customers, and did not see anyone using Excel to actually perform what you would call “calculations.” Almost all of them were using Excel because it was a convenient way to create a table."
From  <https://www.joelonsoftware.com/2012/01/06/how-trello-is-different/>

attachments.title: Rob Fitzpatrick - How to Learn from Customers When Everyone is Lying to You
attachments.fallback: YouTube Video: Rob Fitzpatrick - How to Learn from Customers When Everyone is Lying to You

attachments.title: How Trello is different
attachments.text: Just a few months ago, we launched Trello, a super simple, web-based team coordination system. The feedback has been overwhelmingly positive and adoption has been very strong, even in its early, 1.…
attachments.fallback: Joel on Software: How Trello is different

text: It's a cool story and you've presented it well. I think it's a good name for a language! Building web apps still can be a hassle. What do you see the long term advantages might be? Do you think for web apps you really need a number of DSLS (frontend, routing, persistence, testing, deploying, etc)? What do you think the costs might be to develop the language tooling that would make this a net-win for users?

text:
Are there any languages that make fluid use of linguistic context, as in overloading variables/symbols, type-specific sub-languages/operations/functions? I brainstorm here just because variable names tend to get long and complicated in any language, making thought too verbose. For instance, ‘Maybe Int = Just Int | Nothing’ seems incredibly verbose for something that gets passed around a lot in Haskell.

So if there is anything that makes variables/function names/types easier within specific contexts —at the cost of readability but making experimentation easy — that would be greatly appreciated

text:
seems the paper above is directly claiming emperical evidence doesn't support Papert's constructionist learning theory


<https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Constructionism_(learning_theory)|https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Constructionism_(learning_theory)>

attachments.title: Constructionism (learning theory)
attachments.text:
Constructionist learning is when learners construct mental models to understand the world around them. Constructionism advocates student-centered, discovery learning where students use information they already know to acquire more knowledge. Students learn through participation in project-based learning where they make connections between different ideas and areas of knowledge facilitated by the teacher through coaching rather than using lectures or step-by-step guidance. Further, constructionism holds that learning can happen most effectively when people are active in making tangible objects in the real world. In this sense, constructionism is connected with experiential learning and builds on Jean Piaget's epistemological theory of constructivism.Seymour Papert defined constructionism in a proposal to the National Science Foundation titled Constructionism: A New Opportunity for Elementary Science Education as follows:

The word constructionism is a mnemonic for two aspects of the theory of science education underlying this project. From constructivist theories of psychology we take a view of learning as a reconstruction rather than as a transmission of knowledge. Then we extend the idea of manipulative materials to the idea that learning is most effective when part of an activity the learner experiences as constructing a meaningful product.
Some scholars have tried to describe constructionism as a "learning-by-making" formula but, as Seymour Papert and Idit Harel say at the start of Situating Constructionism, it should be considered "much richer and more multifaceted, and very much deeper in its implications than could be conveyed by any such formula."Papert's ideas became well known through the publication of his seminal book Mindstorms: Children, Computers, and Powerful Ideas (Basic Books, 1980). Papert described children creating programs in the Logo educational programming language. He likened their learning to living in a "mathland" where learning mathematical ideas is as natural as learning French while living in France.

elements.text: seems the paper above is directly claiming emperical evidence doesn't support Papert's constructionist learning theory
https://en.m.wikipedia.org/wiki/Constructionism_(learning_theory)

text: In my digital scientific notation Leibniz (<https://github.com/khinsen/leibniz>), I have tried to imitate the habits of the mathematical/scientific literature: every text introduces its whole notation explicitly, without any defaults nor any reuse of notation from other texts. If you want to write anything in Leibniz, you have to compose your linguistic context explicitly. There are no hierarchical namespaces either, intentionally, to discourage the building of large reusable libraries that nobody can fully understand anymore.

attachments.title: khinsen/leibniz
attachments.text: A Digital Scientific Notation. Contribute to khinsen/leibniz development by creating an account on GitHub.

text:
Hi everyone!
I am the twin brother <@UUB7RA7PF> mentioned above :smile: Similar as Martin, coming from the algorithmic background but also spent a lot of time in full-stack web development (React & Node most lately). Currently working on <https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp> - a DSL for building web apps. Very early and experimental, but looking forward to learn from the community here.

In my free time I like to exercise (calisthenics) and read. Also Vim lover :smile:

attachments.title: wasp-lang/wasp
attachments.text: A programming language that understands what a web app is. - wasp-lang/wasp

elements.text:
Hi everyone!
I am the twin brother
mentioned above
Similar as Martin, coming from the algorithmic background but also spent a lot of time in full-stack web development (React & Node most lately). Currently working on

- a DSL for building web apps. Very early and experimental, but looking forward to learn from the community here.

In my free time I like to exercise (calisthenics) and read. Also Vim lover

text:
Thanks <@UMVFWPZ36>!

> long term advantages
Long term advantage would be code that is closer to specification, shorter and more maintainable. Also, easier to write, lowering the knowledge barier needed to build a web app and allowing faster prototyping.

> you really need a number of DSLS
I am not sure what exactly you mean by this, so pls correct me if I did not answer the question.
Idea of Wasp is that you get a lot of stuff done in a single DSL, that spans the concept of the whole web app. However, if there is something you need to customize in more details, and there is a good way out there to do it that people love, it probably makes more sense to do it that way then to reinvent the wheel in Wasp. Example of that is describing styling -> css is made for that and although it has its issues, people use it, and it would be too big task for us to reinvent it, so Wasp will not try to replace css, but instead allow using it. Same goes for html/jsx for layouting, React/Vue for reusable components, Javascript/TS for general / business logic.
Wasp as DSL serves the purpose of gluing this all together, removing unneeded complexity and hiding ugly/boring parts.

> language tooling
Good question! Since we are imagining it as a pretty horizontal DSL, I believe it will be very important to have a good way to discover options, so some kind of auto completion could help a lot. With LSP I am imagining this should not be so hard to develop for a couple of main editors, but I have never been developing language tooling so it is all a guess :).

I hope these answers are addressing the questions well - I would love to hear your opinions on these topics!

text:
I have been working on/with a new interaction model for livecoding for a while, and just put together the first comprehensive summary of the approach:
<https://s-ol.nu/alivecoding>
Feedback (both to text + content) very welcome!

text: When I started thinking about this I thought that this tagging idea was going to break down at the latest when it gets to function calls or loops, but it turns out it actually works perfectly (tracking changes to function invocation and in the body of the definition, as well as closures etc) without losing any local state when reevaluating changes

text:
My lesson learned over many years is this: the things we are all proposing here are hard enough in their substance to even explain to others, let alone persuade them. We really have no leeway to be unique snowflakes about minor details of presentation. You really need to preserve your readers' cognitive resources for the core argument you're making.

Put another way: if I see something weird in your doc, it better be super critical to your doc. Otherwise I'm definitely gonna bounce.

The name of the game is becoming hard to dismiss.

Ok, I just took another look at the article on the big screen and it makes a lot more sense to me now. Sorry, I'm a little sleep deprived so my comment above is harsher than usual.

I understand now that the critical thing to look at in the video is the grey numbers in square brackets. How are you managing that integration? What editor + tools is it using? And what is the moon tool on the right side? Is that part of what you built or some existing system?

text: The `benefits` section isn't clear to me. Is it a pro and con list between two alternatives? Is the `dataflow` your approach, and the `textual representation and user-controlled evaluation moment` referring to previous approaches that simulate live-coding with an internal scratch buffer? If so, using the same words in different sections would help make these connections in my mind.

text:
<@UCUSW7WVD> the tool on the right is all of what I built. On the left there is a text editor (kakoune) - it is entirely stock except for syntax highlighting that tones down the tags a bit visually.

The tool on the right is evaluating (and modifying) the file whenever it is saved. You can find a more in-depth demo of an earlier version in this video: <https://youtu.be/ZXqgFb1U7q0|https://youtu.be/ZXqgFb1U7q0>

The code is public (<https://git.s-ol.nu/alivecoding|https://git.s-ol.nu/alivecoding>) but heavily wip. At this stage I'm trying to get feedback on the concept and some of the presentation, the project itself is not at the right status for proper sharing and distribution IMO.

text:
languages which can overwrite code like Lisp can of course overload symbols, and is well known to be one of the most flexible languages around. Postscript also allows for redefinition of symbols freely. But the king of brevity in terms of languages is perennially FORTH, whose programmers take delight in rewriting the meaning of + temporarily, and the results are incredibly impressive in both speed and compactness. The problem with FORTH is that it is basically a write-only language, and it is super hard to understand your own code a month later, much less other people's code, because you have to keep in your mind what is on the stack, and how many operators are consumed by each operand. Another language which is highly flexible is Red/Rebol.

In my efforts to develop my Beads language i have moved in the complete opposite direction so that any feature which makes things harder to read is removed, and the whole thrust is to make programs minimal while promoting clarity. For example + in JS means add OR could mean string concat; i use + for add, and & for concat so you can't mix them by mistake (or cause ruinous implied type conversions which JS does so readily).
